excerpt: Professional forecasters predict 1.9% GDP growth for 2026, with significant variance among predictions. The consensus outlook is lower than the top forecasts, which average 2.5%. The outlook for inflation is expected to be 2.9%.

The consensus outlook for real GDP growth in 2026 is 1.9%, but it seems that forecasters have considerable disagreement on the outlook for the year ahead, as reflected in the following:. *   The average of the top 10 GDP growth rate forecasts is 2.5%, while the average of the bottom 10 forecasts is 1.2%.
For 2026, professional forecasters expect 1.9% GDP growth and a 2.9% increase in the consumer price index. Suggested Citation. Charles S. Gascon, 2025. "
Abstract: So far, the 2025 forecasts have turned out to be rather accurate. For 2026, professional forecasters expect 1.9% GDP growth and a 2.9%

Professional forecasters expect 1.9% GDP growth and 2.9% CPI increase in 2026, with notable uncertainty among predictions. The consensus outlook for real GDP growth in 2026 is 1.9%, but there's significant disagreement among forecasts. The ECB Survey of Professional Forecasters also projects 1.2% GDP growth for 2026.

# Revisiting Professional Forecasters’ Past Performance and the Outlook for 2026

  

Abstract:  So far, the 2025 forecasts have turned out to be rather accurate. For 2026, professional forecasters expect 1.9% GDP growth and a 2.9% increase in the consumer price index.

Economy Museum [...] The middle three rows of the table below report the forecasts for 2026. The consensus outlook for real GDP growth in 2026 is 1.9%, but it seems that forecasters have considerable disagreement on the outlook for the year ahead, as reflected in the following:

   The average of the top 10 GDP growth rate forecasts is 2.5%, while the average of the bottom 10 forecasts is 1.2%.
   Some forecasters see the unemployment rate rising while others predict it will fall.
   Similarly, some forecasters predict CPI inflation will accelerate, while others predict it will slow.

In the survey round for the first quarter of 2026, respondents expected the unemployment rate to be 6.3% for 2026, 6.2% for 2027 and 6.1% for 2028. Compared with the previous survey round, the expected trajectory of the unemployment rate was unchanged for 2026 and 2027 (Chart 10). Respondents continued to expect a largely stable and resilient labour market in the euro area, although some expressed concerns that signs of a slight weakening in labour market conditions have started to emerge. The SPF expectations this round were somewhat above those in the December 2025 Eurosystem staff macroeconomic projections: 0.1 percentage points higher for 2026 and 2027, and 0.2 percentage points higher for 2028. Longer-term expectations for the unemployment rate (for 2030) were 6.1%, 0.1 percentage [...] real GDP growth (and risk assessment) was negative and much lower in absolute size for the baseline impact but broadly similar for the risk assessment. [...] GDP growth expectations stood at 1.2% for 2026, 1.4% for 2027 and 1.3% for 2028 (Chart 6). Compared with the previous round, these figures implied a slight upward revision of 0.1 percentage points for 2026, while the outlook for 2027 remained broadly unchanged. The figures for 2026 and 2027 were in line with the December 2025 Eurosystem staff macroeconomic projections, while the figure for 2028 was 0.1 percentage points lower. The longer-term growth expectations (referring to 2030) were unchanged at 1.3%.
